text: In this video vignette, we present complete mesocolic excision technique with two operational approaches according to the tumor location. As the tumor location moves distally, incidence of lymph node metastasis to infrapyloric region, inferior of pancreas, and root of middle colic artery increases. Therefore, dissection of the region above the superior mesenteric vein is crucial in distally located right-sided colon tumors.

In this video vignette, we present complete mesocolic excision technique with two operational approaches according to the tumor location. As the tumor location moves distally, incidence of lymph node metastasis to infrapyloric region, inferior of pancreas, and root of middle colic artery increases. Therefore, dissection of the region above the superior mesenteric vein is crucial in distally located right-sided colon tumors.

The surgeon uses the same trocar placement for both approaches, performing a medial to lateral dissection for cecum and proximal ascending colon tumors. The mesocolic dissection begins from the medial side, where the ileocolic vein is dissected, clipped, and divided at its connection to the superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic artery is similarly dissected, clipped, and divided. Following the dissection of the ileocolic vessels, the mesocolon is carefully separated from the duodenal surface.

The surgeon uses the same trocar placement for both approaches, performing a medial to lateral dissection for tumors located in the cecum and proximal ascending colon. The mesocolic dissection begins from the medial side.

The surgeon dissects, clips, and divides the ileocolic vein at its connection to the superior mesenteric vein, followed by the dissection, clipping, and division of the ileocolic artery. Subsequently, the mesocolon is carefully separated from the duodenal surface.

The surgeon transects the ileum approximately ten centimeters from the ileocecal valve. The middle colic vessels are identified, dissected, clipped, and divided. Indocyanine green dye is used to assess the perfusion of the colon. The transverse colon is transected using robotic staplers, completing the resection.

The surgeon transects the ileum at ten centimeters from the ileocecal valve. The middle colic vessels are identified, dissected, clipped, and divided, completing the vascular dissection in the region.
Indocyanine green dye is used to assess the perfusion of the colon. The transverse colon is transected using robotic staplers, marking the completion of the resection.

The surgeon employs a top-to-down no-touch technique to address tumors in the distal ascending hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon. The dissection begins with the division of the omentum from the stomach, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon employs a top-to-down no-touch technique on distal ascending hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon tumors. The omentum is divided from the stomach, revealing the pancreas. Dissection continues inferior to the pancreas, exposing the superior mesenteric vein. The middle colic vein and gastroepiploic artery are dissected, clipped, and divided. The gastrocolic trunk is identified, dissected, clipped, and divided along the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon visualizes the pancreas and continues dissection inferiorly, revealing the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon dissects, clips, and divides the middle colic vein and gastroepiploic artery. Continuing dissection along the superior mesenteric vein, the gastrocolic trunk is identified, dissected, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon reaches the middle colic artery, which is dissected, clipped, and divided. Using the top-to-down no-touch technique, lymph nodes in the infrapyloric region and around the pancreas are safely dissected within the intact mesocolon. The mesocolon is separated from the duodenum, and the hepatic flexure is mobilized. The superior mesenteric vein is dissected from the pancreas down to the ileocolic vein, with potential vascular injuries being repaired in a controlled manner. Superior dissection is completed, and the ileocolic vessels are identified from the medial side, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon reaches the middle colic artery, which is dissected, clipped, and divided. Using the top-to-down no-touch technique, lymph nodes in the infrapyloric region and around the pancreas are safely dissected within an intact mesocolon. The mesocolon is separated from the duodenum.
The surgeon takes down the hepatic flexure and dissects the superior mesenteric vein from the pancreas down to the ileocolic vein, navigating the complex vascular anatomy.
Following the completion of superior dissection, the surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels from the medial side. The vessels are then clipped and divided, marking progress in the procedure.

Indocyanine green is used to check the perfusion of the colon, which is then transected with a robotic stapler. The ileum is also transected, and the colon is freed from its lateral attachments, completing the resection. For intracorporeal anastomosis, the ends of the ileum and colon are aligned, and two openings are created for the robotic stapler. A side-to-side anastomosis is performed with the stapler, and the remaining openings are closed intracorporeally with a V-like suture.

The surgeon checks the perfusion of the colon using indocyanine green, then transects the colon with a robotic stapler. Following perfusion assessment, the ileum is transected. The colon is freed from its lateral attachments, completing the resection.

The surgeon brings the ends of the ileum and colon together for intracorporeal anastomosis. Two openings are created for the robotic stapler, which is used to perform a side-to-side anastomosis. The remaining openings are then closed intracorporeally with a V-like suture.
